title: Present Simple
description: The foundation of English — used for habits, facts, and routines.
rules: Use for daily routines: "I wake up at 7 AM."
Use for general truths: "The sun rises in the east."
Add -s/-es for he/she/it: "He plays tennis."
Use do/does for questions and negatives.

examples.sentence: She works in a hospital.
examples.explanation: Third person singular — add -s to the verb.

examples.sentence: Do they live in Hanoi?
I don't eat meat.

title: Present Continuous
description: Describe actions happening right now or temporary situations.
rules: Form: am/is/are + verb-ing
Use for actions happening now: "I am studying."
Use for temporary situations: "She is staying with friends."
Some verbs (know, like, want) are NOT used in continuous form.

examples.sentence: They are watching a movie.
Is it raining outside?
He isn't working today.

title: Past Simple
description: Talk about completed actions at a specific time in the past.
rules: Regular verbs: add -ed (worked, played, watched).
Irregular verbs must be memorized (went, saw, bought).
Use with time expressions: yesterday, last week, in 2019.
Questions: Did + subject + base verb?

examples.sentence: We visited Paris last summer.
She didn't come to the party.
Did you enjoy the concert?

title: Present Perfect
description: Connect the past with the present — experiences and recent events.
rules: Form: have/has + past participle
Use for life experiences: "I have visited Japan."
Use for recent actions with present relevance: "She has just arrived."
Time expressions: ever, never, already, yet, just, since, for.

examples.sentence: I have lived here for 5 years.
examples.explanation: Action started in past, continues to present.

examples.sentence: Have you ever tried sushi?
He hasn't finished his homework yet.

title: Conditionals (If clauses)
description: Express hypothetical situations and their consequences.
rules: Zero Conditional: If + present, present — general truths.
First Conditional: If + present, will — real future possibility.
Second Conditional: If + past, would — unreal present/future.
Third Conditional: If + past perfect, would have — unreal past.

examples.sentence: If it rains, I will stay home.
If I were rich, I would travel the world.
If I had studied harder, I would have passed.

title: Articles (a / an / the)
description: Choose the correct article — a common challenge for Vietnamese learners.
rules: Use "a" before consonant sounds: a book, a university (yoo- sound).
Use "an" before vowel sounds: an apple, an hour (silent h).
Use "the" for specific or known things: the book on the table.
No article for general plural/uncountable nouns: I like music. Cats are cute.

examples.sentence: She is an honest person.
examples.explanation: "Honest" starts with a vowel sound — use "an".

examples.sentence: The sun is bright today.
I need a pen.
examples.explanation: "A" — any pen, not a specific one.

title: Modal Verbs
description: Express ability, permission, obligation, and advice with modals.
rules: Can / Could — ability or polite request: "Can you help me?"
Must / Have to — obligation: "You must wear a seatbelt."
Should / Ought to — advice: "You should drink more water."
May / Might — possibility: "It might rain later." Modals are followed by base verb (no "to").

examples.sentence: You should see a doctor.
Can I borrow your pen?
Students must submit homework on time.

title: Future Tenses (will / going to)
description: Talk about plans, predictions, and spontaneous decisions.
rules: Will + base verb — predictions or instant decisions: "I'll call you."
Be going to + base verb — planned intentions: "I'm going to study tonight."
Present Continuous for fixed arrangements: "We're meeting at 3 PM."
Time expressions: tomorrow, next week, soon, in two hours.

examples.sentence: I'm going to visit my parents this weekend.
I think it will rain tomorrow.
Look at those clouds — it's going to rain!

title: Comparatives & Superlatives
description: Compare people, places, and things using -er/-est or more/most.
rules: Short adjectives: add -er / -est (tall → taller → tallest).
Long adjectives: more / most (beautiful → more beautiful → most beautiful).
Irregular: good → better → best; bad → worse → worst.
Use "than" in comparisons: "Hanoi is bigger than Hue." As...as for equality.

examples.sentence: This book is more interesting than that one.
She is the best student in the class.
English is as important as math.

title: Prepositions of Time & Place
description: Master in, on, at and other prepositions Vietnamese speakers often confuse.
rules: Time — at (at 7 PM, at night), on (on Monday, on July 1), in (in 2024, in summer).
Place — at (at the station), on (on the table), in (in Hanoi, in the room).
Movement — to (go to school), from (from Vietnam), into (walk into the shop).
Fixed phrases: interested in, good at, depend on — memorize as chunks.

examples.sentence: I was born in 1995 in Vietnam.
The meeting is on Friday at 2 PM.
She is good at speaking English.

title: Passive Voice
description: Focus on the action or result rather than who performs it.
rules: Form: be + past participle — "The letter was written yesterday."
Use when the doer is unknown or unimportant: "English is spoken worldwide."
By-phrase adds the agent: "The song was written by Taylor Swift."
Common in formal writing, news, and academic English.

examples.sentence: The homework was checked by the teacher.
Rice is grown in many parts of Vietnam.
The project will be completed next month.

title: Reported Speech
description: Report what someone said without quoting their exact words.
rules: Present → Past: "I am tired" → He said (that) he was tired.
Past Simple → Past Perfect: "I saw her" → He said he had seen her.
Will → Would: "I will help" → She said she would help.
Questions and commands change structure: "Where do you live?" → He asked where I lived.

examples.sentence: She told me (that) she was studying English.
examples.explanation: Present Continuous → Past Continuous in reported speech.

examples.sentence: He asked if I could speak Vietnamese.
The teacher told us to open our books.
